content.text: Why Reliability NFRs Are Different for AI Workloads

Standard web service reliability patterns — load balancers, health checks, autoscaling — apply to GenAI systems, but AI workloads introduce failure modes that traditional services do not have. GPU memory fragmentation, model weight loading times measured in minutes, non-deterministic inference latency under load, and provider API rate limits all require AI-specific reliability controls on top of the usual toolkit.

This document defines the reliability and availability NFRs that belong in every GenAI system's architecture specification, explains the reasoning behind each threshold, and shows how to implement the critical mechanisms in code.

attrs.alt: GenAI reliability architecture with multi-region failover and circuit breaker

content.text: Service Level Objectives (SLOs) form a hierarchy: the user-facing SLO sets the ceiling, and each internal dependency gets a tighter budget so that the sum of component failures stays within the user SLO.

Rule of thumb: never promise a GenAI SLO tighter than your model provider's own SLA. OpenAI targets 99.9% for its API. If you wrap it without a fallback, you inherit its outages and cannot exceed its reliability.
An error budget is the permitted downtime: (1 − SLO) × window. A 99.9% monthly SLO gives 43.8 minutes of budget. The error budget policy defines what happens when budget is consumed:
0–50% consumed: normal velocity, feature work proceeds
50–75% consumed: postmortem required for each incident, reliability work prioritized
> 100% consumed: incident escalation, SLO renegotiation if systemic
Each subsystem in a GenAI architecture must carry its own NFR so that downstream teams have concrete targets for their SLAs.
* Provider SLA — not under your control. Your reliability architecture must budget for provider failures.

A circuit breaker sits in front of the external model provider (or any unreliable dependency). It has three states: CLOSED (normal operation), OPEN (failing fast, not sending requests), and HALF_OPEN (testing recovery). This prevents a provider outage from cascading into request queues and timeouts across your entire system.

The circuit breaker wraps every external call. When the provider trips the breaker, your fallback path (cached response, degraded mode, or queued retry) activates immediately rather than stacking timed-out requests.

A simple error-rate alert fires too late — by the time the threshold triggers, the error budget is already heavily consumed. The burn rate approach fires early by measuring how fast the budget is being used relative to normal.
NFRs should be machine-readable so they can be enforced in CI and architecture review tooling. A structured dataclass makes the targets auditable and lets you check conformance automatically.
Warm Pool for GPU Inference Nodes

GPU nodes take 5–15 minutes to provision and load model weights. A warm pool pre-starts a small number of nodes so that autoscaling burst is available within seconds, not minutes. The NFR for scale-out latency should be explicitly defined:
Graceful Degradation Under Provider Rate Limits
When the external model provider rate-limits you, the system must degrade gracefully rather than return 500 errors. The degradation chain defines what to serve at each severity level:
Architecture review boards expect NFRs documented in a structured format. The following template captures reliability requirements per service component:

# reliability-nfrs.yaml
# One block per logical service component in the system

service: genai-inference-api
version: "1.0"
last_reviewed: "2026-01-15"
reviewer: platform-architecture-team

components:

  inference_endpoint:
    slo_availability: "99.9%"
    slo_window: "30d"
    error_budget_minutes_per_window: 43.8
    rto_minutes: 5
    rpo_minutes: 0
    topology: active-passive-multi-region
    circuit_breaker:
      enabled: true
      failure_threshold: 5
      recovery_timeout_s: 30
    degradation_chain:
      - level: cached
        condition: provider_error_rate > 5%
      - level: simplified
        condition: cache_miss AND fallback_model_available
      - level: queued
        condition: queue_depth < 500
      - level: unavailable
        condition: queue_depth >= 500

  vector_store:
    slo_availability: "99.9%"
    slo_window: "30d"
    rto_minutes: 5
    rpo_minutes: 0
    topology: multi-az-replica
    degradation_chain:
      - level: approximate_search
        condition: replica_lag > 30s
      - level: unavailable
        condition: all_replicas_down

  prompt_cache:
    slo_availability: "99.5%"
    topology: redis-replica
    degradation_chain:
      - level: cache_bypass
        condition: redis_unavailable

alerting:
  burn_rate:
    fast_window_m: 5
    slow_window_m: 60
    threshold_multiplier: 14.4
    paging_channel: pagerduty-genai-oncall
  error_budget:
    warn_at_pct: 50
    page_at_pct: 75
    freeze_at_pct: 100
Chaos experiments validate that reliability controls actually work under failure. GenAI systems have additional failure modes that standard chaos toolkits do not cover:
Remaining pods absorb load, no user-facing error
Pod fails health check, replaced automatically
Inject 10s delay on all outbound calls
Scale-out completes within RTO, no SLO breach
DNS cutover, DR active within RTO
Document each chaos experiment as a runbook with a success/failure criterion. A failed chaos drill is a reliability risk; it must trigger the same postmortem process as a production incident.
inference_request_total{status} — counter for success/4xx/5xx; used to compute error rate for burn rate alerts
inference_latency_seconds{quantile} — p50/p95/p99 per model and endpoint; trigger SLO breach detection
warm_pool_nodes{state} — gauge of warm/cold node counts; alert when warm pool < minimum
error_budget_remaining_minutes — derived metric; dashboarded for team visibility
failover_triggered_total{region, reason} — counter of DR activations for trend analysis

Reliability NFRs for GenAI services build on standard SRE practices but must account for AI-specific failure modes: GPU pool exhaustion, model weight loading delays, external provider rate limits, and non-deterministic latency spikes. The critical controls are: SLO targets with explicit error budget policies, component-level availability targets for every dependency, circuit breakers in front of external model providers, multi-window burn rate alerting, warm GPU pools to meet scale-out RTO, and a degradation chain that maintains partial service when primary inference is unavailable. Encoding these NFRs as machine-readable specifications lets architecture review boards enforce them in CI and gives on-call engineers a clear contract for what the system must do under failure.
